query: A person accused under section 327 of POCA 2002 may rely on an authorised disclosure as a defence — which section of POCA governs such disclosures, and what must the disclosure consist of?
answer: Section 333D POCA 2002 governs authorised disclosures. The disclosure must be one of the following: (a) to the authority that is the supervisory authority for that person by virtue of the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017; (aa) for the purposes of proceedings under section 336A (power of court to extend moratorium period); (ab) made in good faith by virtue of section 339ZB (disclosures within the regulated sector); or (b) for the purpose of— (i) the detection, investigation or prosecution of a criminal offence (whether in the United Kingdom or elsewhere), (ii) an investigation under this Act, or (iii) the enforcement of any order of a court under this Act.

query: How does section 335 of POCA 2002 define 'appropriate consent', and when is a person treated as having received it in the absence of an explicit response?
answer: Appropriate consent is the consent of a nominated officer to do a prohibited act if an authorised disclosure is made to the nominated officer. [poca_2002_s335] Appropriate consent is the consent of a constable to do a prohibited act if an authorised disclosure is made to a constable. [poca_2002_s335] Appropriate consent is the consent of a customs officer to do a prohibited act if an authorised disclosure is made to a customs officer. [poca_2002_s335] A person is treated as having the appropriate consent if they make an authorised disclosure to a constable or a customs officer, and the condition in subsection (3) or the condition in subsection (4) is satisfied. [poca_2002_s335] The condition in subsection (3) is that before the end of the notice period he does not receive notice from a constable or customs officer that consent to the doing of the act is refused. [poca_2002_s335] Therefore, if no notice of refusal is received before the end of the notice period, the condition in subsection (3) is satisfied. [poca_2002_s335]

query: MLR 2017 regulation 29 specifies it applies 'in addition to' the measures in another regulation for credit and financial institutions — which base regulation, and what additional obligations does regulation 29 impose regarding insurance policy beneficiaries?
answer: Base regulation: Regulation 28. [mlr_2017_reg_29] Additional obligations for insurance policy beneficiaries under Regulation 29: As soon as the beneficiaries are identified or designated, the relevant person must— (a) if the beneficiary is a named person or legal arrangement, take the full name of the person or arrangement; or (b) if the beneficiaries are designated by specified characteristics, as a class or in any other way, obtain sufficient information about the beneficiaries to satisfy itself that it will be able to establish the identity of the beneficiary before any payment is made under the insurance policy. [mlr_2017_reg_29]

query: MLR 2017 regulation 3 defines 'money laundering' by reference to a specific POCA 2002 provision — which section, and what conduct does that provision describe as money laundering?
answer: Section 21A(2) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (POCA) is the referenced provision. [jmlsg_1_6.7] It describes money laundering as including an attempt to commit an offence under sections 327–329 of POCA. [jmlsg_1_6.7]

query: MLR 2017 regulation 44 requires trustees to maintain records containing information specified in another regulation — which regulation prescribes that information, and what categories of beneficial owner data are required?
answer: Regulation 44 requires trustees to maintain records containing information specified in Regulation 45(2) and (9) (the register of beneficial ownership) [mlr_2017_sch6_para9]. The categories of beneficial owner data are those set out in Regulation 45(2) and (9) [mlr_2017_sch6_para9]. The provided context does not specify the actual data categories themselves [mlr_2017_sch6_para9].

query: Regulation 27(1)(c) of MLR 2017 requires a relevant person to apply CDD where it suspects money laundering — how is money laundering defined for the purposes of the Regulations, and where is that definition found?
answer: Money laundering for the purposes of the Regulations is defined by reference to the money laundering directive, now Directive 2015/849/EU (the Fourth Money Laundering Directive) [mlr_2017_sch7_para28(2)]. The definition is found in Regulation 2(1) (interpretation) of the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, as amended to substitute Directive 2015/849/EU for the previous directive [mlr_2017_sch7_para28(2)]. The Explanatory Note confirms these Regulations replace the Money Laundering Regulations 2007 with updated provisions implementing the Fourth Money Laundering Directive [mlr_2017_sch7_para35].

query: Regulation 28 of MLR 2017 states it applies when a relevant person is required by another regulation to apply CDD measures — which regulation creates that trigger obligation, and in what circumstances does it require CDD to be applied?
answer: Regulation 28(1) of the ML Regulations triggers the obligation to apply CDD measures; Regulation 28 governs when a relevant person must apply CDD where required by another regulation [jmlsg_1_5.3]. The circumstances require CDD to be applied include: verifying the identity of customers and, where applicable, beneficial owners, and assessing the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship (and obtaining information on this where appropriate) [jmlsg_1_5.3]. Identification of the customer is done by obtaining information and verifying some of it against documents or information from a reliable source independent of the customer [jmlsg_1_5.3]. If reliance on a third party is used, Regulation 39 permits it but the relying firm remains responsible for compliance, and Regulation 39 sets out the parties on whom reliance can be placed (including certain regulated firms in non-EEA jurisdictions) [jmlsg_1_5.6.4][jmlsg_2_20.58]. Note that firms relying on third parties to apply CDD should not rely on the third party for monitoring [jmlsg_2_20.66].

query: Regulation 30 of MLR 2017 sets the timing rule for completing verification — to which specific regulations does it apply, and what is the default timing requirement it establishes?
answer: Regulation 30's timing rule operates within the ML Regulations' CDD framework and aligns with Regulation 28's requirements. [jmlsg_1_5.3.136] The default timing requirement is that the verification of the customer's identity (and, where applicable, the beneficial owner) must take place before the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of a transaction. [jmlsg_1_5.2]

query: Section 332 of POCA 2002 creates a failure-to-disclose offence for persons nominated to receive disclosures under sections 337 or 338 — what are the conditions of that offence, and how does it differ from the section 330 offence?
answer: Section 332 creates an offence for a person nominated to receive disclosures under sections 337 or 338 if the conditions in subsections (2) to (4) are satisfied. [poca_2002_s332] The first condition (subsection 2) is that the nominee knows or suspects that another person is engaged in money laundering. [poca_2002_s332] The second condition (subsection 3) is that the information or other matter on which his knowledge or suspicion is based came to him in consequence of a disclosure made under the applicable section. [poca_2002_s332] The third condition (subsection 3A) is that, as a consequence of that disclosure, he knows the identity of the other person, or the whereabouts of any of the laundered property, or that that other person or property can be identified from the information or matter disclosed. [poca_2002_s332] The offence is framed by subsections (2) to (4) being satisfied. [poca_2002_s332] The section 330 offence is a similar provision that applies to a person nominated to receive disclosures under section 330: “A person nominated to receive disclosures under section 330 commits an offence if the conditions in subsections (2) to (4) are satisfied.” [poca_2002_s331] The 330 offence uses the disclosure made under section 330 as the basis for the knowledge/suspicion and for the 3A identification condition, i.e., the same structure as section 332 but tied to the source section 330. [poca_2002_s331] The two offences differ in the source of the disclosure (section 330 for the 330 offence; sections 337 or 338 for the 332 offence), but they share the same core conditions: knowledge or suspicion of money laundering, that the information came from a qualifying disclosure, and the potential to identify the person or laundered property from that disclosure. [poca_2002_s331] [poca_2002_s332]

query: Section 335 of POCA 2002 defines 'appropriate consent' as consent given in response to an authorised disclosure — what does section 338 require for that underlying disclosure to be authorised?
answer: The provided context does not address what Section 338 requires for that underlying disclosure to be authorised. The available material discusses POCA consent in relation to disclosures and money laundering offences (e.g., the NCA’s consent following a disclosure and consent to proceed with a transaction) rather than the specific requirements of Section 338 for authorising an underlying disclosure. [poca_2002_s339ZG] [jmlsg_1_6.76] [poca_2002_s339ZF]

query: The failure-to-disclose offence in POCA 2002 section 330 applies where a person knows or suspects another is engaged in money laundering — how is 'money laundering' defined in section 340 for this purpose?
answer: The provided context does not answer how 'money laundering' is defined in section 340 for this purpose. [jmlsg_1_6.7] The context does, however, indicate that 'money laundering' is defined in s21A(2) POCA to include an attempt to commit an offence under s327-329 of POCA. [jmlsg_1_6.7]

query: The section 327 offences in POCA 2002 apply to 'criminal property' — where in POCA is that term defined, and what two elements must be established to show that property qualifies as criminal?
answer: The provided context does not contain the definition of 'criminal property' in POCA or specify the two elements needed to establish that property qualifies as criminal.

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 27, in what circumstances is a relevant person required to apply customer due diligence measures?
answer: Under Regulation 27, the circumstances include (i) determining when it is appropriate to undertake customer due diligence on a prospective customer, and (ii) ensuring that appropriate customer due diligence is completed once a binding customer relationship has been established.[jmlsg_2_14.24]

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 29, when must a credit institution or financial institution identify the beneficiary of a long-term insurance policy?
answer: Under MLR 2017 regulation 29, as soon as the beneficiaries of the insurance policy are identified or designated, the credit institution or financial institution must identify them: (i) if the beneficiary is a named person or legal arrangement, take the full name; or (ii) if the beneficiaries are designated by specified characteristics as a class or in any other way, obtain sufficient information about the beneficiaries to satisfy itself that it will be able to establish the identity of the beneficiary before any payment is made under the insurance policy. [mlr_2017_reg_29]

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 4, at what point is an estate agent considered to have entered into a business relationship with a purchaser?
answer: Under Regulation 4, a business relationship is deemed to exist at the time contact is established if the relationship arises out of the relevant person's business and is expected, at that time, to have an element of duration [mlr_2017_reg_4].

query: Under section 331 of POCA 2002, who specifically commits an offence by failing to disclose knowledge or suspicion of money laundering, and what are the conditions of that offence?
answer: Under POCA 2002 s331, the offence is committed by a person nominated to receive disclosures under section 330. [poca_2002_s331] The conditions of the offence are: 1) The first condition is that he knows or suspects, or has reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering. [poca_2002_s331] 2) The second condition is that the information or other matter on which his knowledge or suspicion is based, or which gives reasonable grounds for such knowledge or suspicion, came to him in consequence of a disclosure made under section 330. [poca_2002_s331] 3A The third condition is that he knows the identity of the other person mentioned in subsection (2), or the whereabouts of any of the laundered property, in consequence of a disclosure made under section 330. [poca_2002_s331] 4) The fourth condition is not shown in the provided text; the context references subsections (2) to (4) but subsection (4) content is not included here. [poca_2002_s331]

query: Under section 336 of POCA 2002, what conditions must be satisfied before a nominated officer may give appropriate consent to a prohibited act?
answer: Under section 336 of POCA 2002, a nominated officer must not give the appropriate consent to the doing of a prohibited act unless the condition in subsection (2), the condition in subsection (3) or the condition in subsection (4) is satisfied [poca_2002_s336]. The conditions shown in the text are: Condition (2): he makes a disclosure that property is criminal property to a person authorised for the purposes of this Part by the Director General of the National Crime Agency, and such a person gives consent to the doing of the act [poca_2002_s336]. Condition (3): he makes a disclosure that property is criminal property to a person authorised for the purposes of this Part by the Director General of the National Crime Agency, and before the end of the notice period he does not receive notice from such a person that consent to the doing of the act is refused [poca_2002_s336]. Subsection (4) is not shown in the provided context [poca_2002_s336].

query: What authorised disclosure under POCA 2002 can prevent the section 328 arrangement offence from being committed, and which section sets out the requirements for that disclosure?
answer: The provided context does not address section 328 arrangement offence. However, within the provided material, an authorised disclosure that can prevent the corresponding offence is described as: a disclosure to the authority that is the supervisory authority for that person by virtue of the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017; ab disclosure made in good faith by virtue of section 339ZB (disclosures within the regulated sector); or b disclosure for the purposes of the detection, investigation or prosecution of a criminal offence (whether in the United Kingdom or elsewhere), an investigation under this Act, or the enforcement of any order of a court under this Act. The section that sets out the requirements for that disclosure is section 339ZB. [poca_2002_s333D]

query: What information must a credit institution gather about a third-country respondent institution under MLR 2017 regulation 34 before establishing a correspondent relationship?
answer:
- Gather sufficient information about the respondent to understand fully the nature of its business [mlr_2017_reg_34].
- Determine from publicly available information from credible sources the reputation of the respondent and the quality of the supervision to which the respondent is subject [mlr_2017_reg_34].
- Assess the respondent’s controls to counter money laundering and terrorist financing [mlr_2017_reg_34].
- Obtain approval from senior management before establishing a new correspondent relationship [mlr_2017_reg_34].
- Document the responsibilities of the respondent and the correspondent [mlr_2017_reg_34].

query: What must a credit institution apply under MLR 2017 regulation 34 in addition to the measures required by regulation 33 when establishing a correspondent relationship with a third-country institution?
answer:
- Gather sufficient information about the respondent to understand fully the nature of its business. [mlr_2017_reg_34]
- Determine from publicly-available information from credible sources the reputation of the respondent and the quality of the supervision to which the respondent is subject. [mlr_2017_reg_34]
- Assess the respondent’s controls to counter money laundering and terrorist financing. [mlr_2017_reg_34]
- Obtain approval from senior management before establishing a new correspondent relationship. [mlr_2017_reg_34]
- Document the responsibilities of the respondent and corresponden. [mlr_2017_reg_34]

query: What three conditions must all be satisfied for a person to commit the failure-to-disclose offence under section 330 of POCA 2002?
answer: The provided context does not answer the question about the failure-to-disclose offence under section 330 [poca_2002_s333A]. However, the three conditions that must be satisfied for the offence described in section 333A are: 1) the person discloses any matter within subsection (2); [poca_2002_s333A] 2) the disclosure is likely to prejudice any investigation that might be conducted following the disclosure referred to in that subsection; [poca_2002_s333A] 3) the information on which the disclosure is based came to the person in the course of a business in the regulated sector. [poca_2002_s333A]
